column2: The Eastwick Urban Renewal Area is a 2,506-acre redevelopment project in Southwest Philadelphia, run by the Philadelphia Redevelopment Authority since 1958, that is transforming former marsh and substandard land near the Schuylkill and Delaware Rivers into a planned 'New Town' within the city. The plan covers four residential stages and three industrial stages, including row houses, apartments, shopping centers, schools, churches, parks, and a proposed Mini-Town Center near Island Avenue and Lindbergh Boulevard. Land acquisition is complete, about 1,550 acres remain to be conveyed, and the Ne…

column2: The 1970 Census counted about 15,977 residents in Eastwick (roughly 87% white), with 5,006 housing units and a median family income of $9,283; full buildout is projected to bring the population to about 33,000. New residents tend to be younger, middle and upper-middle income, and more racially mixed than the older established community, which lives mainly in row houses north of the project. Since 1959 the Authority has relocated about 2,400 families, 325 individuals, and 207 businesses, and the Eastwick Project Area Committee, representing eight neighborhood and business groups, provides orga…

Three broad alternatives were weighed: doing nothing (rejected because the area would remain marshy and substandard), expanding clearance and rehabilitation (limited to avoid added relocation and cost), and changing land uses in problem areas. The most significant change is converting Stage III from residential to airport-related commercial use because of flooding, air quality, and aircraft noise, with HUD tentatively approving the change in 1971 and a 50-acre motel-commercial complex recommended. Other options considered include raising grades above the 100-year flood, building dikes to prot…

The project eliminates marshy lowlands and wildlife habitat, contributes sediment to Darby and Cobbs Creeks and the adjacent Tinicum Marsh Preserve (with over four feet of siltation at the Tinicum flood gates since 1969), and leaves parts of the area exposed to 100-year tidal flooding at about elevation 10–11 feet, requiring proposed dikes and surge basin changes. Noise from aircraft (NEF 30–40 contours), I-95 and other major roads, and the Reading Railroad and Airport High Speed Line requires soundproofing, setbacks, and 20–35 dBA noise reduction in construction, while air pollution analyses…

Federal agencies (Interior, EPA, Army Corps of Engineers, DOT/FAA, Commerce) and state agencies (PennDOT, PA DER) raised concerns about creek sedimentation, ground water and air quality, historic resources such as the John Bartram House, and the wisdom of building more housing inside airport noise contours, with several urging major changes to Stages III and IV. Local bodies including the City Planning Commission, SEPTA, and the Korman Corporation generally supported continued development with mitigation and backed the Stage III commercial conversion, while the Redevelopment Authority dispute…
